paper_analysis.evidence_text: “For a pair of input samples x_L and x_T, we obtain their representation features z_L, z_T and the fusion feature z_F… For a class-specific expert k, its weight w_k is given by: … represent as the class center… The overall loss is formulated as follows, where L_CE is the standard Cross-Entropy Loss. L_CMCL is the contrastive loss from Eq. (3) and λ is the balancing coefficient.” “Let D_train={x_L^i, x_T^i, y_i}… update the memory bank M… update process is as follows: where α∈[0,1] is the momentum coefficient …”
paper_analysis.notes: The excerpts indicate variable definitions, dataset notation, EMA update, loss components, and a gating/weighting mechanism. However, some formulas are truncated in the provided text (e.g., explicit expressions for w_k and EMA update), so completeness cannot be fully verified. Given likely full paper includes equations (Eq. (3) referenced), I mark present but with moderate confidence.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: Previous contrastive learning methods perform positive and negative comparisons using only samples within a large batch to better approximate the global distribution... [We] introduce the CMCL, which allows features of the same class to cluster toward a stable center in the feature space while pushing apart features from different classes globally...
paper_analysis.notes: While the paper describes mechanisms (memory bank, EMA, parameter-free MoE gating) and hyperparameters, it does not explicitly state modeling assumptions about data distribution, independence between views, stationarity, or convergence conditions. Statements about clustering around class centers are design intuitions, not explicit assumptions.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: No explicit mention of software framework (e.g., PyTorch, TensorFlow, scikit-learn) or version is found in the provided sections.
paper_analysis.page_reference: Experimental Results; Abstract; DSAM module description
paper_analysis.notes: The provided excerpts discuss dataset, metrics, modules, and results but do not specify any implementation framework or version. Full paper might include it elsewhere, but it's absent in the given text.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: "All images were resized to 224×224... randomly split into 7:1:2... Pretrained weights from ImageNet... trained for 100 epochs... using AdamW optimizer with a learning rate of 1e-4... The EMA momentum coefficient α, CMCL temperature Coefficient τ , and loss Weight λ are set to 0.01, 0.01, and 0.2, respectively. All hyperparameters are determined through experiments on the validation set."
paper_analysis.notes: They report LR, optimizer (AdamW), epochs, image size, split, EMA momentum, contrastive temperature, loss weight, pretrained backbone, and hardware. Batch size and some optimizer settings (e.g., weight decay, betas) are not explicitly stated, but substantial hyperparameters are provided.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: "The EMA momentum coefficient α, CMCL temperature Coefficient τ , and loss Weight λ are set to 0.01, 0.01, and 0.2, respectively. All hyperparameters are determined through experiments on the validation set."
paper_analysis.notes: They explicitly state using the validation set to determine hyperparameters, satisfying the requirement of describing the selection method (validation set tuning).

paper_analysis.evidence_text: "Table . 1 presents a comparison of CVC-RF with single-view methods, multi-view feature fusion and weighting approaches..." and the table lists baseline names and metrics (e.g., Dual-ResRet18, ETMC, DeepGuide, MVC, CheXFusion).
paper_analysis.notes: While multiple baselines are listed and compared, there are no details on how these baselines were implemented or tuned (architectural settings, preprocessing, training schedules, hyperparameters, or whether authors reimplemented or used official code). Hence fairness and reproducibility of baseline comparisons cannot be assessed.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: "Table . 1 presents a comparison of CVC-RF with single-view methods, multi-view feature fusion and weighting approaches, as well as the results of ablation experiments for each module... the removal of the DSAM or MoE module led to a performance drop... ablating CMCL resulted in a significant 5.54% drop in Acc."
paper_analysis.notes: An ablation study for DSAM, MoE, and CMCL is reported, showing impact of removing components. However, no hyperparameter sensitivity (e.g., λ, τ, EMA α) sweeps are shown. Still satisfies criterion via design-choice ablations.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: "Table 1 . 1,657 pairs of carotid US images (518 RADS1, 772 RADS2, 367 RADS3-4) from 1,228 patients, with each patient contributing no more than two cases... All images were resized to 224×224 and randomly split into 7:1:2 for training, validation, and testing."
paper_analysis.notes: They report dataset size, class distribution, number of patients, split, and resizing. However, key statistics like patient demographics (age/sex), imaging parameters (scanner/probe, acquisition settings), and other relevant demographics are missing. Thus, only partially satisfied; per rubric requiring demographics and imaging parameters, mark as not satisfied.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: "All images were resized to 224×224 and randomly split into 7:1:2 for training, validation, and testing."
paper_analysis.notes: They provide a random 7:1:2 train/val/test split ratio. They do not specify exact fold definitions or whether multiple random seeds/splits were used, but the split strategy is clearly stated.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: "We collected a private dataset comprising Quantitative and Qualitative Analysis." and "1,657 pairs of carotid US images... This study was approved by the local IRB."
paper_analysis.page_reference: Experimental Results; Table 1 / Dataset and Implementations
paper_analysis.notes: The paper uses a private, IRB-approved dataset. No public dataset names are mentioned, and no instructions on how to access or request the data are provided.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: "This study was approved by the local IRB."
paper_analysis.notes: They used a private clinical dataset and explicitly state IRB approval.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: All results were evaluated using paired t-tests with p-values<0.05. Table . 1 presents a comparison... The CVC-RF was implemented... trained for 100 epochs...
paper_analysis.notes: The paper reports metrics and statistical tests, but does not state how many independent training/evaluation runs (seeds) were performed. No mention of repeats or variance/standard deviation across runs.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: We evaluate the performance of CVC-RF using accuracy (Acc), mean precision (M-Pre), mean recall (M-Rec), mean F1-score (M-F1), and per-class accuracy (Acc1-3). All results were evaluated using paired t-tests with p-values<0.05. Table . 1 presents a comparison...
paper_analysis.notes: Metrics are reported as single percentages without ± standard deviations or confidence intervals. Although t-tests are mentioned, no variance measures across runs are provided in the table or text excerpts.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: "All results were evaluated using paired t-tests with p-values<0.05."
paper_analysis.notes: They explicitly mention using paired t-tests with p<0.05 to assess results. Specific p-values per comparison are not listed in the provided excerpt, but the statement indicates statistical testing was performed.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: "We evaluate the performance of CVC-RF using accuracy (Acc), mean precision (M-Pre), mean recall (M-Rec), mean F1-score (M-F1), and per-class accuracy (Acc1-3)."
paper_analysis.notes: Metrics are listed but no clear definitions or formulas (e.g., how mean over classes is computed, F1 definition, macro vs micro) are provided. No formulas for Dice/IoU/AUC etc. Thus criterion of clear metric definitions with formulas is not met.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: "The model infers in 41.96 ms/case, with 18.73M parameters, 16.50 GFLOPs, demonstrating strong deployment potential."
paper_analysis.notes: Inference time per case is reported along with parameters and FLOPs. Training time is not reported, but inference runtime suffices to satisfy this criterion.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: "The CVC-RF was implemented using the PyTorch framework and trained for 100 epochs on an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090 GPU... The model infers in 41.96 ms/case, with 18.73M parameters, 16.50 GFLOPs"
paper_analysis.notes: They report GPU model, parameters, FLOPs, and inference time, but do not specify GPU memory (VRAM) or RAM usage required to train or run the method. Hence memory footprint is not provided.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: The provided sections report performance gains, ablations, dataset/setup, and metrics but contain no discussion of failure cases, limitations, error analysis, or scenarios where the method underperforms.
paper_analysis.notes: No qualitative/error examples, per-class confusion, or analysis of misclassified cases is presented in the supplied text. If present elsewhere (e.g., Discussion/Supplement), it is not included here.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: "The model infers in 41.96 ms/case... demonstrating strong deployment potential." and "Accurate carotid plaque grading (CPG) is crucial for early diagnosis and prevention of stroke."
paper_analysis.notes: While the introduction motivates clinical relevance of CPG and Table 1 mentions inference speed/deployment potential, the experimental results do not discuss practical/clinical significance of improvements (e.g., impact on patient triage, clinician workflow, decision thresholds, or minority class implications in clinical terms). Improvements are reported as percentages with p<0.05 but without clinical interpretation.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: "The CVC-RF was implemented using the PyTorch framework and trained for 100 epochs on an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090 GPU... The model infers in 41.96 ms/case, with 18.73M parameters, 16.50 GFLOPs"
paper_analysis.notes: GPU model is specified (RTX 4090). Number of GPUs, CPU model, and system memory are not reported. Still, at least partial hardware specs (GPU) are provided.
